passages.explanation: Be careful not to do your righteous deeds before people to be seen by them — otherwise you have no reward from your Father in heaven. When you give to the needy, don't sound a trumpet before you like the hypocrites do in the synagogues and streets so they may be praised. When you give, don't let your left hand know what your right hand is doing, so that your giving may be in secret. Your Father who sees in secret will reward you openly. The word 'hypocrite' literally means actor — someone performing for an audience. Jesus reframes the audience for all religious practice: it's not the crowd, it's God.

When you pray, don't be like the hypocrites who love to stand and pray in the synagogues and on street corners to be seen by people. Go into your inner room, shut the door, and pray to your Father who is in secret. Don't use vain repetitions like the Gentiles do — they think they'll be heard for their many words. Your Father knows what you need before you ask him. Prayer isn't about informing God or impressing people. It's about honest relationship with a Father who already knows.

Jesus gives the model prayer: Our Father in heaven, may your name be kept holy. Let your Kingdom come. Let your will be done on earth as it is in heaven. Give us today our daily bread. Forgive us our debts, as we also forgive our debtors. Don't bring us into temptation, but deliver us from the evil one. For yours is the Kingdom, the power, and the glory forever. Then Jesus adds a warning: if you forgive others, your heavenly Father will forgive you. If you don't forgive others, neither will your Father forgive you. The Lord's Prayer covers everything — worship, submission, provision, forgiveness, and protection. The forgiveness clause isn't optional.

When you fast, don't look gloomy like the hypocrites — they disfigure their faces so people will see they're fasting. When you fast, anoint your head and wash your face, so you don't appear to people to be fasting, but to your Father who is in secret. Your Father who sees in secret will reward you. The pattern is the same as giving and prayer: do it before God, not before people. If your spiritual discipline needs an audience to feel meaningful, you've already received your full reward.

Don't lay up treasures for yourselves on earth, where moth and rust consume and thieves break in and steal. Lay up treasures in heaven instead. Where your treasure is, there your heart will be also. The lamp of the body is the eye — if your eye is good, your whole body will be full of light. If it's bad, your whole body will be full of darkness. No one can serve two masters — you'll hate one and love the other, or be devoted to one and despise the other. You can't serve both God and Mammon. Jesus treats money as a rival deity, not just a neutral tool. The question isn't how much you have — it's what has you.

Therefore, don't be anxious about your life — what you'll eat, drink, or wear. Look at the birds of the sky: they don't sow or reap or gather into barns, yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Aren't you of more value than they are? Which of you by being anxious can add one moment to his lifespan? Consider the lilies of the field — they don't toil or spin, yet Solomon in all his glory wasn't dressed like one of these. If God so clothes the grass of the field, won't he much more clothe you, you of little faith? Don't be anxious, saying what will we eat or wear — the Gentiles seek all these things. Your Father knows you need them. Seek first God's Kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well. Don't worry about tomorrow — tomorrow will worry about itself. Each day has enough trouble of its own. This isn't a call to irresponsibility. It's a call to reorder priorities. When the Kingdom comes first, anxiety loses its grip.
